# Questions in Livestream 3

Live Stream #3: Thrice the Bugs, Thrice the Charm.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=164" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44</a> | What primary documents on ancient Egypt would you recommend for high school history students? | He recommends starting with a broad historical overview, such as Ian Shaw's works, because Egyptian history is too large to reduce to one definitive document. | He says Egypt is too vast for one source to serve as the obvious starting point. For students, he recommends beginning with a reliable overview that can orient them before moving into translated primary texts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=291" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:51</a> | How did you derive the 2312 date for Sargon? | He says the date comes from computer-based chronology modeling, which can process king lists and synchronisms more consistently than hand-built chronologies. | He explains that the date comes from a computer model that evaluates chronological data, reign lengths, and synchronisms. He trusts that approach more than hand calculations because it can process many constraints consistently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:40</a> | What are your thoughts on the origins of Gnosticism? Is it a distinctly Egyptian phenomenon? | He describes Gnosticism as pre-Christian and as a synthesis of Egyptian religion and Neoplatonic thought that could re-allegorize other religions. | He frames Gnosticism as older than Christianity and not simply a Christian heresy. In his account it draws on Egyptian religious ideas and Neoplatonic categories, then reinterprets other traditions through that symbolic and allegorical framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=697" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:37</a> | Where in Ramesses II's reign do you think the exodus happened, and what evidence supports that view? | He places it around Ramesses II's 25th year, after the construction of Pi-Ramesses and near the death of the crown prince, with the Merneptah Stele setting an outer boundary. | He places the event after Pi-Ramesses had been built and before Merneptah's campaign could mention Israel in Canaan. The death of Ramesses' crown prince and the chronological room before the Merneptah Stele help him locate the exodus around year 25. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=943" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:43</a> | Is there historical evidence for a pharaoh having two queens at once? | Kings could have many wives, but only one Great Royal Wife at a time; succession and political marriages explain the larger royal harem. | He distinguishes the Great Royal Wife from the larger royal harem. Pharaoh could have many wives for succession, diplomacy, or household reasons, but the formal office of Great Royal Wife was normally singular. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=1184" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:44</a> | Was Kadesh of the exodus near Petra/Wadi Musa or near Ein el-Qudeirat? | He is reserving judgment; the location of Kadesh-Barnea remains debated, and recent arguments have reopened the question. | He does not commit to either identification. He notes that older consensus favored Ein el-Qudeirat, but newer arguments have reopened the Petra/Wadi Musa possibility, so he leaves the question unsettled. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=1362" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:42</a> | Were the Shasu Israelites? If not, how widespread was worship of Yahweh? | No. He says the Shasu predate the exodus and were nomadic groups known mostly from Egyptian lists; the spread of Yahweh worship remains uncertain. | He says the Shasu were not Israelites as such, because they appear in Egyptian sources before Israel can be identified in that way. The Shasu of Yhw may be relevant to Yahweh's southern associations, but it does not establish how widespread Yahweh worship was. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=1590" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:30</a> | Where do you think Mount Sinai is? | He does not claim a precise location, but thinks it was likely among mountains in southern Sinai; the evidence excludes some options more than it identifies one site. | He says the evidence narrows the region more than it identifies a single mountain. He leans toward southern Sinai and away from proposals that do not fit the route and landscape constraints he sees in the biblical itinerary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=1832" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:32</a> | Did you see Holy Koolaid's video, and will you do a review? | He saw it but says he probably will not review it, because he views it as a distorted treatment rather than a serious work. | He says he watched the video but does not think it merits a full review. His reason is not that he cannot answer it, but that he sees the presentation as too distorted to be worth extended engagement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=2057" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:17</a> | Is there evidence that Semites in Egypt worshiped golden calves? | He says not necessarily golden calves specifically, but there is broad evidence for bovine worship, and the golden calf episode fits a default Egyptian-style bovine worship pattern. | He distinguishes a specific golden-calf artifact from a broader bovine cult pattern. Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean had strong bull and cow symbolism, so he sees the golden calf episode as a plausible reversion to familiar bovine deity worship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=2255" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:35</a> | Did the Israelite army of Moses have a forced retirement age like the Levites? | He thinks the army was more a practical muster of who could fight than a professional force with formal retirement rules. | He treats the Israelite force as a militia-style muster rather than a standing professional army. The Levite age limits applied to service duties, while fighting capacity would have been judged more practically. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=2376" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:36</a> | What is your favorite evidence for the exodus events? | He highlights the Merneptah Stele, the abandonment of Avaris, linguistic and toponymic evidence, and ritual-furniture parallels with the Ark of the Covenant. | He presents the case as cumulative: Merneptah gives an outside anchor for Israel in Canaan, Avaris fits a Semitic population departure, Egyptian language and place names fit the setting, and Ark parallels support an Egyptian ritual-furniture context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=2748" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:48</a> | How do secular scholars explain Egyptian loanwords in the Pentateuch, and why do they not see them as evidence for the exodus? | He says they often explain them through Egyptian influence in Canaan, but he thinks that requires too many assumptions and does not fit the broader linguistic pattern well. | He says Egyptian control in Canaan is often used to explain the loanwords without an exodus. He thinks that explanation becomes strained when the vocabulary, geography, and distribution of Egyptian terms are considered together. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=3038" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:38</a> | Are there similarities between the tabernacle layout and Pharaoh's war-camp tent? | Yes. He says Ramesside reliefs show similar tent-within-a-tent patterns, but sees military tents as influenced by older religious tabernacle structures. | He acknowledges formal similarities between Ramesside military camp reliefs and the tabernacle layout. But he reverses the common assumption, suggesting military royal tents borrowed from older sacred tent traditions rather than the tabernacle copying a war camp. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=3246" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:06</a> | How many scholars accept the existence of the Shasu, and if they accept the Shasu, why do they not accept the biblical exodus? | He says the Shasu and the people who became Israelites are separate issues; evidence for the Shasu is straightforward, while pre-Merneptah Israelite identity is harder to define archaeologically. | He says almost no one disputes the Shasu because Egyptian texts clearly mention them. The exodus question is different: identifying Israelites before the Merneptah Stele is harder because the people would be archaeologically and ethnically difficult to distinguish. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=3500" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:20</a> | Do you take 1290 or 1279 as the beginning of Ramesses II's reign? | Neither. He prefers about 1288 BC, while noting that 1279 is still an acceptable date held by other scholars. | He says he prefers a slightly higher date around 1288 BC, but he does not treat 1279 as fringe or impossible. The disagreement reflects chronology-model choices rather than a simple factual certainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=3672" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:12</a> | Does the bird imagery in Jesus' lament over Jerusalem connect winged sun disks to Yahweh and Christ? | He sees a protective-wing motif behind the passage, related to Psalm 91 and winged imagery such as cherubim and Egyptian vulture symbolism, with some connection to winged sun disks. | He traces the image through protective wings rather than a single direct borrowing. The motif appears in cherubim, Psalm 91, Egyptian vulture imagery, and winged disk symbolism, all of which can inform how divine protection is pictured. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=4037" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:17</a> | Does Joshua have Egyptian toponyms and loanwords like the Pentateuch? | Yes, but less so; he says Egyptian vocabulary diminishes through Joshua, Judges, Kings, and the prophets as later linguistic influences increase. | He says Joshua still contains Egyptian elements, but the density drops compared with the Pentateuch. That tapering pattern matters to him because later books increasingly show other linguistic influences, matching a changing historical setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=4220" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:20</a> | Do the Brooklyn Slave Papyrus or Ipuwer Papyrus provide a timeframe for the exodus? | No. The Brooklyn Papyrus has Semitic names used across a long period, and he dates Ipuwer's language to the 12th Dynasty, so neither narrows the exodus date. | He says both sources are relevant background but poor chronological anchors. The Brooklyn names are too broadly used to date the exodus, and Ipuwer's language belongs too early to function as a direct timestamp for the event. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=4377" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:57</a> | How could heavy Greek or Sea Peoples influence appear in the tribe of Dan? | He connects it to Dan's difficulty securing its inheritance and later settlement among regions with Neo-Hittite and Anatolian/Sea Peoples cultural influence. | He ties the issue to Dan's movement and settlement history. If Dan struggled to hold its allotted territory and relocated northward, contact with Neo-Hittite, Anatolian, and Sea Peoples-influenced regions could explain unusual cultural signals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=4536" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:36</a> | Do people dig specifically for manuscripts, and is there hope of finding more Christian papyri soon? | People generally do not dig only for manuscripts, but he says more Christian papyri may still appear, especially from the large unpublished Oxyrhynchus material. | He says excavations are usually not mounted solely to find manuscripts, though papyri can be discovered in the right contexts. He especially points to the huge body of Oxyrhynchus material as a place where more Christian texts may emerge from publication work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=4879" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:19</a> | What sources do you recommend on Nebuchadnezzar's conquest of Egypt? | He points to Mesopotamian and Babylonian annals rather than Egyptian sources, because Egyptian records generally avoid reporting defeats. | He says Egyptian records are usually poor witnesses to Egyptian defeats because they tend not to preserve humiliating losses. For Nebuchadnezzar's campaigns, he recommends looking to Mesopotamian and Babylonian sources instead. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=5000" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:20</a> | Was Egyptian *sDm.n=f* one verbal form, or did it hide two differently vocalized forms? | He accepts a second-tense use in line with Polotsky, but says the evidence does not let us reconstruct distinct pronunciations securely. | He accepts the grammatical distinction associated with second tense analysis, but separates that from pronunciation. Because Egyptian writing does not preserve vowels well enough, he says the evidence cannot securely recover two distinct vocalized forms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=5253" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:33</a> | What are the origins of the ancient Egyptians? Were they originally from the Levant or Mesopotamia? | He says we do not know; migration from Mesopotamia is possible but unproven, and he favors caution because predynastic evidence is limited. | He refuses a confident origin story because the predynastic evidence is limited and complex. Mesopotamian influence or movement is possible, but he treats it as unproven and prefers caution. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aauuHFdpbuU?t=5474" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:14</a> | What do you consider the blood in Exodus to be, regarding the Nile turning to blood? | He says ancient hearers would think of black, foul, undrinkable water rather than bright red liquid; the plague amplifies familiar Nile inundation phenomena into something abnormal. | He says "blood" should not be pictured only as bright red liquid. In the Egyptian Nile setting, the plague evokes foul, dark, undrinkable water and intensifies familiar inundation phenomena into a destructive sign. |
